terms.definition: Biomicroscope projecting a focused slit of light, used to examine the anterior segment and, with adjuncts, the posterior segment.
terms.exampleSentence: Slit lamp examination revealed a few cells in the anterior chamber and 2+ flare.
terms.contextNotes: Cornerstone instrument for every optometric exam.

terms.definition: Hand-held or head-mounted instrument used to inspect the optic disc, retina and fundus.
terms.exampleSentence: Direct ophthalmoscopy through an undilated pupil showed a healthy disc.
terms.contextNotes: Indirect BIO offers wider field with stereopsis.

terms.definition: Hand-held instrument that projects light into the eye to objectively determine refractive error from the reflex movement.
terms.exampleSentence: Retinoscopy gave a starting point of -2.50 DS that was refined subjectively.
terms.contextNotes: Essential for paediatric and non-verbal patients.

terms.definition: Refraction instrument containing rotating lens wheels presented before the patient's eyes during subjective refraction.
terms.exampleSentence: Best subjective refraction through the phoropter gave 6/6 acuity.
terms.contextNotes: Trial frame preferred for high prescriptions and prism.

terms.definition: Automated device that objectively estimates refractive error using infrared light reflected from the retina.
terms.exampleSentence: The autorefractor reading was used as a starting point before subjective refinement.
terms.contextNotes: Useful screen but never replaces subjective refraction.

terms.definition: Instrument used to measure intraocular pressure as part of glaucoma assessment.
terms.exampleSentence: Tonometer readings of 24 mmHg in both eyes prompted referral for glaucoma assessment.
terms.contextNotes: Adjust IOP for central corneal thickness.

terms.definition: Slit-lamp-mounted applanation tonometer regarded as the clinical gold standard for measuring intraocular pressure.
terms.exampleSentence: Goldmann tonometry gave an IOP of 18 mmHg in the right eye after instillation of fluorescein.
terms.contextNotes: Recalibrate weekly; correct for corneal thickness.

terms.definition: Hand-held rebound tonometer that measures intraocular pressure without anaesthetic, useful for children and home monitoring.
terms.exampleSentence: The iCare tonometer was preferred for the anxious paediatric patient.
terms.contextNotes: Tends to read slightly higher than GAT in some eyes.

terms.definition: Non-contact imaging modality using low-coherence interferometry to produce micron-scale cross-sections of the retina and anterior segment.
terms.exampleSentence: Optical coherence tomography revealed cystoid macular oedema with central thickening of 410 microns.
terms.contextNotes: Standard for AMD, glaucoma and DMO monitoring.

terms.definition: Automated perimeter that maps the sensitivity of the visual field to detect characteristic patterns of loss.
terms.exampleSentence: Visual field analysis showed an early superior arcuate defect respecting the horizontal midline.
terms.contextNotes: Reliability indices must be checked on every print-out.

terms.definition: Brand of automated static perimeter widely used for glaucoma and neuro-ophthalmic field assessment.
terms.exampleSentence: Humphrey field analysis using the 24-2 SITA Standard programme detected an early nasal step.
terms.contextNotes: 10-2 grid is preferred for advanced field loss.

terms.definition: Eye chart of progressively smaller letters used to measure distance visual acuity at a standardised distance.
terms.exampleSentence: Snellen acuity was 6/9 in the right eye and 6/12 in the left.
terms.contextNotes: Calibrated at 6 m or with appropriate scaling.

terms.definition: Acuity chart with logarithmic letter size progression and equal letters per row, preferred for research and low-vision work.
terms.exampleSentence: Best-corrected LogMAR acuity improved from 0.4 to 0.1 after cataract surgery.
terms.contextNotes: Score letter-by-letter for sensitivity to small changes.

terms.definition: Pseudo-isochromatic plates used to screen for red-green congenital colour vision deficiency.
terms.exampleSentence: He read 1/17 Ishihara plates correctly, consistent with red-green colour deficiency.
terms.contextNotes: Use under standardised illuminant C lighting.

terms.definition: Square grid pattern used to detect central visual field distortion and scotomas, primarily in macular disease.
terms.exampleSentence: Distortion of central lines on the Amsler grid prompted urgent macula clinic referral.
terms.contextNotes: Dispense to AMD patients for daily home monitoring.

terms.definition: Mirrored contact lens used at the slit lamp to visualise the iridocorneal angle for grading and pathology.
terms.exampleSentence: A four-mirror gonioscopy lens revealed pigment in the trabecular meshwork.
terms.contextNotes: Mandatory before peripheral iridotomy.

terms.definition: Examination of the fundus, including the optic disc, retina and vessels, using direct or indirect ophthalmoscopy.
terms.exampleSentence: Dilated fundoscopy was performed using tropicamide 1%.
terms.contextNotes: Document disc, vessels, macula and periphery.

terms.definition: Process of measuring the eye's refractive error to prescribe corrective lenses.
terms.exampleSentence: Subjective refraction yielded a prescription of -1.75/-0.50 x 175 in the right eye.
terms.contextNotes: Cycloplegic refraction is essential in young hyperopes.

terms.definition: Examination of the entire posterior pole and periphery after pharmacological pupil dilation.
terms.exampleSentence: Dilated fundus examination identified a small horseshoe tear in the superotemporal periphery.
terms.contextNotes: Warn drivers about transient blur and photophobia for 4-6 hours.

terms.definition: Adjustable spectacle frame holding loose lenses for refraction, especially with high prescriptions or paediatric patients.
terms.exampleSentence: A trial frame check confirmed the patient tolerated the new astigmatic correction.
terms.contextNotes: Better than phoropter for prism and high astigmatism.

terms.definition: Device that measures central corneal thickness, important for IOP correction and refractive surgery planning.
terms.exampleSentence: Pachymetry of 480 microns suggested the IOP reading was an under-estimate.
terms.contextNotes: Thin corneas under-estimate Goldmann IOP.

terms.definition: Instrument that measures the curvature of the anterior cornea, used in contact lens fitting and IOL calculations.
terms.exampleSentence: Keratometer readings of 44.00/45.50 @ 90 supported a contact lens base curve of 8.4.
terms.contextNotes: Inadequate alone in irregular astigmatism — use topography.

terms.definition: Application of sodium fluorescein dye to the ocular surface to highlight epithelial defects under cobalt blue light.
terms.exampleSentence: Punctate fluorescein staining inferiorly was consistent with exposure keratopathy.
terms.contextNotes: Avoid in patients wearing soft contact lenses.

terms.definition: Clinical test alternately occluding each eye to detect heterotropia and heterophoria.
terms.exampleSentence: Cover-uncover test revealed a small left exotropia in primary gaze.
terms.contextNotes: Always test with appropriate accommodative target.

terms.definition: Multi-hole occluder used to determine whether reduced acuity is refractive or pathological in origin.
terms.exampleSentence: Acuity improved through the pinhole occluder, suggesting an uncorrected refractive component.
terms.contextNotes: No pinhole improvement is a soft sign for retinal/optic nerve disease.
